faq.question: How do I calculate square footage?
faq.answer: Multiply length by width. For irregular shapes, break into rectangles.

faq.question: How many square feet in an acre?
Is the Square Footage Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard construction and building formulas trusted by contractors and DIY builders. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What length should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate length value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

contentIntro: Square footage calculations are the foundation of almost every home improvement and real estate transaction, yet errors are surprisingly common. Irregular room shapes, closets, hallways, and multi-level spaces all complicate what seems like simple math. For real estate purposes, only finished, heated living space counts — garages, unfinished basements, and covered porches are excluded from official square footage. For material estimates like flooring or paint, you need to measure differently than for appraisals. This calculator handles rectangular, L-shaped, and irregular rooms and converts between square feet, square yards, and square meters.
howToSteps: Measure and enter the length and width of each rectangular section of the room
For L-shaped rooms, break the space into two rectangles and enter each separately
Subtract any areas you want to exclude — columns, built-in fixtures, or areas you are not covering
View the total square footage along with conversions to square yards and square meters
For flooring projects, add 10% waste factor to your total to account for cuts and mistakes
tipsAndConsiderations: Measure to the nearest inch, not foot. On a 12x15 room, being off by 6 inches on each wall changes your area by almost 14 square feet — enough to need an extra box of flooring.
Real estate square footage should be measured from the exterior walls for appraisals, not interior dimensions. Interior measurements give you usable space, which is 5-10% less.
For flooring material, always add 10% for waste on standard layouts and 15% for diagonal or herringbone patterns.
Ceiling height matters too. A 1,500 sq ft home with 10-foot ceilings feels significantly larger than the same footprint with 8-foot ceilings, and costs more to heat and cool.
tips: Double-check your length before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both square feet and square meters — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to plan your projects.
If you're unsure about your width, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
